<script type="text/javascript">
var maindomain_lang = 'https://www.paloaltonetworks.com';
var main_site_url = 'https://www.paloaltonetworks.com';
var ajax_url = 'https://unit42.paloaltonetworks.com/wp-admin/admin-ajax.php';	
function getParameterByName(name, url) {
		if(url == null){
		  url = window.location.href;
		}
	    name = name.replace(/[\[\]]/g, '\\$&');
	    var regex = new RegExp('[?&]' + name + '(=([^&#]*)|&|#|$)'),
		results = regex.exec(url);
	    if (!results) return null;
	    if (!results[2]) return '';
	    return decodeURIComponent(results[2].replace(/\+/g, ' '));
	}
	var container_q = getParameterByName('container');
	var d_lang = 'en';	
</script>

<meta name="post_tags" content="Muddled Libra,PowerShell,Scattered Spider,UNC3944,virtual machines"/>
<script type="application/ld+json">{"@context":"https:\/\/schema.org","@type":"BlogPosting","headline":"A Peek Into Muddled Libra\u2019s Operational Playbook","name":"A Peek Into Muddled Libra\u2019s Operational Playbook","description":"Explore the tools Unit 42 found on a Muddled Libra rogue host. Learn how they target domain controllers and use search engines to aid their attacks.","url":"https:\/\/unit42.paloaltonetworks.com\/muddled-libra-ops-playbook\/","mainEntityOfPage":"https:\/\/unit42.paloaltonetworks.com\/muddled-libra-ops-playbook\/","datePublished":"February 10, 2026","articleBody":"Executive Summary\r\nDuring a September 2025 incident response investigation, Unit 42 discovered a rogue virtual machine (VM) which we believe with high confidence to be used by the cybercrime group Muddled Libra (aka Scattered Spider, UNC3944). The contents of this rogue VM and activity from the attack provide valuable insight into the operational playbook of this threat actor.\r\n\r\nMuddled Libra created the VM after the group successfully gained unauthorized access to the target's VMware vSphere environment. Activities during the attack include:\r\n\r\n \tPerforming reconnaissance\r\n \tDownloading tools\r\n \tEstablishing persistence via a command and control (C2) channel\r\n \tUsing stolen certificates\r\n \tCopying files from the rogue VM to the target's domain controller (DC)\r\n \tInteracting with the target\u2019s Snowflake infrastructure\r\n\r\nBased on the characteristics of the attack, we assess with high confidence that Muddled Libra conducted it. This article provides a detailed analysis of our observations to shed further light on the threat actor\u2019s tactics, techniques and procedures (TTPs).\r\n\r\nPalo Alto Networks are better protected from the threats discussed in this article through the following products and services:\r\n\r\n \tAdvanced WildFire\r\n \tCortex Cloud\r\n \tCortex XDR and XSIAM\r\n\r\nIf you think you might have been compromised or have an urgent matter, contact the Unit 42 Incident Response team.\r\n\r\n\r\n\r\nRelated Unit 42 Topics\r\nMuddled Libra, Cybercrime\r\n\r\n\r\n\r\nWho Is Muddled Libra?\r\nAs previously documented, threat actors affiliated with Muddled Libra use various social engineering tactics (e.g., smishing, vishing) to gain initial access to targeted organizations. Activities can include targeting call centers operated by potential victims, as well as those outsourced to third-party firms. These third-party firms include business process outsourcing (BPOs) and managed service providers (MSPs). This expands the group\u2019s range of potential targets.\r\n\r\nThreat actors affiliated with Muddled Libra are highly proficient at exploiting human psychology by impersonating employees to attempt password and multi-factor authentication (MFA) resets. Figure 1 illustrates the composition of Muddled Libra in terms of their demographics, tradecraft, victim targeting and actions on objectives.\r\n\r\n[caption id=\"attachment_172387\" align=\"alignnone\" width=\"1000\"] Figure 1. Muddled Libra threat profile.[\/caption]\r\n\r\nWhile their tradecraft has evolved, threat actors affiliated with Muddled Libra continue to minimize their use of malware throughout the attack chain. Whenever possible, they prefer to use their targets' own assets against them.\r\n\r\nThreat actors often abuse, take advantage of or subvert legitimate products for malicious purposes. This does not imply that the legitimate product is flawed or malicious.\r\nBackground on the Attack Chain\r\nWe assisted a client with an incident response engagement in September 2025. Throughout our investigation, we identified and recovered a VM created and leveraged by the threat actor to conduct the early stages of its attack. Attackers were unable to delete this VM before their access was cut off. We often observe threat actors creating VMs within targeted environments to avoid detection from endpoint tools like endpoint detection and response (EDR) or extended detection and response (XDR).\r\n\r\nBy examining this VM, we discovered the tools the attackers leveraged and the basic troubleshooting they conducted during their unauthorized access. This provided insights into their operational methods.\r\n\r\nUsing forensic artifacts and logs, we uncovered a large amount of activity conducted from this VM, including lateral movement and tools used. Figure 2 further illustrates our observations during the investigation. Note that the analysis below is of a single system and does not cover the entire incident observed by Unit 42.\r\n\r\n[caption id=\"attachment_172398\" align=\"alignnone\" width=\"1000\"] Figure 2. High-level chain of events in the attack investigated by Unit 42.[\/caption]\r\nA Peek Into Muddled Libra Tactics\r\nApproximately two hours after gaining initial access to the target\u2019s environment, we observed the attackers accessing the target\u2019s vSphere portal and creating a new VM named \u201cNew Virtual Machine.\u201d The attackers then leveraged this VM for the early stages of the incident as a beachhead host using the local Administrator account.\r\n\r\nShortly after logging into the newly created VM, attackers downloaded stolen certificates from the targeted environment. They leveraged these certificates to forge tickets throughout their attack chain.\r\n\r\nWithin three minutes, attackers established additional persistence in the target\u2019s environment using an SSH tunnel through the Chisel tool. This tool was contained in a ZIP archive named goon.zip that was hosted on an AWS S3 bucket under the attackers' control.\r\n\r\nNearly one minute after they downloaded the ZIP archive containing Chisel, we observed malicious network connections to an attacker-controlled IP address over TCP port 443 (HTTPS). We observed this connection for a total of 15 hours. Figures 3-5 illustrate these observations.\r\n\r\n[caption id=\"attachment_172409\" align=\"alignnone\" width=\"1000\"] Figure 3. URL hosting archive containing the Chisel tool.[\/caption]\r\n\r\n[caption id=\"attachment_172420\" align=\"alignnone\" width=\"1000\"] Figure 4. The downloaded tool, goon.zip.[\/caption]\r\n\r\n[caption id=\"attachment_172431\" align=\"alignnone\" width=\"1000\"] Figure 5. The SSH tunneling tool, chisel.exe, extracted from goon.zip.[\/caption]\r\n\r\nA minute later, we observed them logging in interactively with a new local user account named gooner.\r\n\r\nApproximately 15 minutes after creating the VM, the attackers began using vSphere to power down two of the target's virtualized DCs. They then mounted the virtual machine disks (VMDKs) of the powered-down DCs. This allowed them to copy the NTDS.dit and SYSTEM registry hive files from these two DCs and place them on the desktop of the Administrator account on their newly created VM.\r\n\r\nApproximately two minutes later, they wrote two files, result and result.kerb, to the local Administrator account\u2019s desktop. We retrieved these files and determined that these were decrypted versions of the target\u2019s NTDS.dit Active Directory database, which contained hashes of all users. Figures 6 and 7 illustrate these observations.\r\n\r\n[caption id=\"attachment_172442\" align=\"alignnone\" width=\"1000\"] Figure 6. VMware logs of the shutdown activities of the DC.[\/caption]\r\n\r\n[caption id=\"attachment_172453\" align=\"alignnone\" width=\"1000\"] Figure 7. List of files discovered for credential dump, NTML hash and Kerberos hash.[\/caption]\r\n\r\nAt nearly 30 minutes of access to this newly created VM, the attackers began executing the Active Directory enumeration tool ADRecon. We observed and retrieved dozens of files associated with ADRecon, including a PowerShell script and output files.\r\n\r\nThese files contained information such as:\r\n\r\n \tDomain details\r\n \tForest\r\n \tTrusts\r\n \tSites\r\n \tSubnets\r\n \tSchema\r\n \tPassword policy\r\n \tDCs\r\n \tService Principal Names (SPNs)\r\n \tUsers\r\n \tGroup Policy Objects (GPOs)\r\n\r\nThe output of the ADRecon tool would then be placed in a ZIP archive named &lt;VICTIM ORGANIZATION&gt;.zip (where &lt;victim organization&gt; represents the name of the victim, redacted for this report). We also observed the attackers downloading the tool ADExplorer64.exe directly from the Microsoft SysInternals domain. Figure 8 illustrates these observations.\r\n\r\n[caption id=\"attachment_172464\" align=\"alignnone\" width=\"1000\"] Figure 8. List of ADRecon output files discovered during our investigation.[\/caption]\r\n\r\nWithin the ADRecon output, the threat actors only opened the CSV file ComputerSPNs.csv. This file contained all available service principal names (SPNs) associated with hosts in the environment. Attackers gather this information to help identify critical services running that they are interested in targeting. These critical services include:\r\n\r\n \tVeeam\r\n \tTerminal services\r\n \tHyper-V\r\n \tMSSQL\r\n \tExchange\r\n \tOther similar systems as shown in Figure 9\r\n\r\n[caption id=\"attachment_172475\" align=\"alignnone\" width=\"1000\"] Figure 9. List of targeted services discovered during our investigation.[\/caption]\r\n\r\nOne hour later, attackers began searching the web for various acronyms associated with the targeted organization, likely to determine what data could be sensitive and interesting for exfiltration. This included searches such as \u201cwhat is NAIC code\u201d and \u201cNAICS code lookup,\u201d as shown in Figure 10.\r\n\r\nA North American Industry Classification System (NAICS) code is a six-digit number that classifies businesses by their primary economic activity. By looking up this code, attackers might have been trying to understand the business category of the target organization.\r\n\r\n[caption id=\"attachment_172486\" align=\"alignnone\" width=\"451\"] Figure 10. Example of web searches.[\/caption]\r\n\r\nThirty minutes after their web searches, attackers began interacting with significant data from the target\u2019s Snowflake database, which they also downloaded to their VM. For the next few hours, attackers began interacting with the data and attempting to identify ways to send the data from their VM to a file-sharing site. However, we observed them having difficulties finding a file-sharing site that the targeted organization had not already blocked.\r\n\r\nAfter trying several common file-sharing sites, they began using Bing to search on the phrases \u201cupload files\u201d and \u201cupload files no registration\u201d to identify a file-sharing site that was not blocked. We observed attempts at accessing sites such as:\r\n\r\n \tLimeWire\r\n \tupload[.]ee\r\n \tuploadnow[.]io\r\n \tfiletransfer[.]io\r\n \tfilebin[.]io\r\n \tDropbox\r\n\r\nFigures 11-13 illustrate these observations.\r\n\r\n[caption id=\"attachment_172497\" align=\"alignnone\" width=\"1000\"] Figure 11. List of Snowflake web browsing activities documented from our investigation.[\/caption]\r\n\r\n[caption id=\"attachment_172508\" align=\"alignnone\" width=\"549\"] Figure 12. Web searches for cloud storage services discovered during our investigation.[\/caption]\r\n\r\n[caption id=\"attachment_172519\" align=\"alignnone\" width=\"550\"] Figure 13. Web browsing activities to cloud storage services discovered during our investigation.[\/caption]\r\n\r\nShortly after interacting with the data, the attackers began lateral movement using multiple then-compromised accounts with their SSH tunnel, RDP and PsExec. They downloaded the PsExec tool directly from the Microsoft SysInternals domain.\r\n\r\nApproximately four hours after the creation of their VM, the attackers began looking for additional sensitive data. At that time, we observed them having compromised a handful of accounts, one of which they used to access the mailboxes of other accounts to download a Personal Storage Table (PST) file. However, based on Bing search history, they had difficulty accessing the mailbox via Office.\r\n\r\nThe attackers then searched the web for \u201coffice[.]com old setup download\u201d and \u201cis there a place to download an older version of Outlook?\u201d Attackers also reviewed various Reddit posts related to this query. Shortly after, they downloaded and ran OfficeSetup.exe from Microsoft.\r\n\r\nThey then began troubleshooting items such as \u201cOutlook slow downloading emails\u201d and performed several internet speed checks using the site fast[.]com. Figures 14-17 illustrate these observations.\r\n\r\n[caption id=\"attachment_172530\" align=\"alignnone\" width=\"1000\"] Figure 14. Online Outlook login activities discovered during our investigation.[\/caption]\r\n\r\n[caption id=\"attachment_172541\" align=\"alignnone\" width=\"1000\"] Figure 15. Web searches for Outlook agents discovered during our investigation.[\/caption]\r\n\r\n[caption id=\"attachment_172552\" align=\"alignnone\" width=\"1000\"] Figure 16. Web searches for older Outlook versions discovered during our investigation.[\/caption]\r\n\r\n[caption id=\"attachment_172563\" align=\"alignnone\" width=\"626\"] Figure 17. Web searches to troubleshoot slow email download issues and perform a speed test.[\/caption]\r\n\r\nIn addition to troubleshooting their download speeds, attackers used Bing to identify the location of the Outlook ODT file. Figure 18 illustrates this observation.\r\n\r\n[caption id=\"attachment_172574\" align=\"alignnone\" width=\"457\"] Figure 18. Web search for the Outlook ODT file location discovered during our investigation.[\/caption]\r\n\r\nAdditionally, we observed Microsoft Defender, presumably installed on the VM by default, taking action on multiple malicious files on this VM. This included ADRecon, Chisel and GoSecretsDump. Figure 19 lists these detections.\r\n\r\n[caption id=\"attachment_172585\" align=\"alignnone\" width=\"1000\"] Figure 19. Microsoft Defender detections on tools discovered during our investigation.[\/caption]\r\n\r\nAfter some time, the attackers then began attempting to exfiltrate the Outlook PST file. They first searched for the S3 Browser tool. Once they downloaded the tool from the S3 Browser website, attackers then attempted to exfiltrate the PST file by uploading it to their S3 bucket. Figures 20 and 21 illustrate these observations.\r\n\r\n[caption id=\"attachment_172596\" align=\"alignnone\" width=\"1000\"] Figure 20. Downloaded S3 browser executable discovered during our investigation.[\/caption]\r\n\r\n[caption id=\"attachment_172607\" align=\"alignnone\" width=\"1000\"] Figure 21. S3 Browser logs of PST file upload.[\/caption]\r\n\r\nAfter approximately 15 hours of access to their VM, attackers began browsing various VMware ESXi hosts. They continued to pivot through the network, leveraging access to multiple compromised accounts before their access was terminated by the targeted organization\u2019s security team. Figure 22 illustrates this observation.\r\n\r\n[caption id=\"attachment_172618\" align=\"alignnone\" width=\"1000\"] Figure 22. Browsing activities to the target\u2019s VMware ESXi hosts discovered during our investigation.[\/caption]\r\nConclusion\r\nIntrusion operations Muddled Libra conducts have affected the business operations of many organizations across the globe. This is not because they use advanced malware or novel exploits, but because they exploit the weakest link: humans.\r\n\r\nWhile focusing on identity compromise and social engineering, this threat actor leverages legitimate tools and existing infrastructure to blend in. They operate quietly and maintain persistence.\r\n\r\nThis incident offers a rare window into an operational playbook used by Muddled Libra, revealing how a single rogue VM can serve as a powerful foothold for lateral movement and data theft. The threat actor's methods underscore the importance of:\r\n\r\n \tStrengthening identity security\r\n \tEnforcing strict access controls\r\n \tContinuously monitoring for anomalous use of administrative tools and cloud environments\r\n\r\nOrganizations should adopt a defense-in-depth strategy centered on:\r\n\r\n \tProtecting identity\r\n \tMaintaining least-privileged access\r\n \tDetecting living-off-the-land behaviors\r\n\r\nWhile Muddled Libra\u2019s tactics may appear simple, their effectiveness reminds us that cybersecurity resilience begins not with complexity, but with vigilance, visibility and disciplined access management.\r\n\r\nPalo Alto Networks customers are better protected from the threats discussed above through the following products:\r\n\r\nThe Advanced WildFire machine-learning models and analysis techniques have been reviewed and updated in light of the indicators shared in this research.\r\n\r\nCortex Cloud customers can better protect their cloud infrastructure from the topics discussed within this article through the proper placement of Cortex Cloud XDR endpoint agent and serverless agents within their cloud environment. Designed to protect a cloud\u2019s posture and runtime operations against these threats, Cortex Cloud helps detect and prevent the malicious operations or configuration alterations or exploitations discussed within this article.\r\n\r\nCortex XDR and XSIAM help to prevent the threats described in this blog, by employing the Malware Prevention Engine. This approach combines several layers of protection, including WildFire, Behavioral Threat Protection and the Local Analysis module, designed to prevent both known and unknown malware from causing harm to endpoints.\r\n\r\nIf you think you may have been compromised or have an urgent matter, get in touch with the Unit 42 Incident Response team or call:\r\n\r\n \tNorth America: Toll Free: +1 (866) 486-4842 (866.4.UNIT42)\r\n \tUK: +44.20.3743.3660\r\n \tEurope and Middle East: +31.20.299.3130\r\n \tAsia: +65.6983.8730\r\n \tJapan: +81.50.1790.0200\r\n \tAustralia: +61.2.4062.7950\r\n \tIndia: 000 800 050 45107\r\n \tSouth Korea: +82.080.467.8774\r\n\r\nPalo Alto Networks has shared these findings with our fellow Cyber Threat Alliance (CTA) members. CTA members use this intelligence to rapidly deploy protections to their customers and to systematically disrupt malicious cyber actors. Learn more about the Cyber Threat Alliance.\r\nIndicators of Compromise\r\nHost Based\r\nImportant note: The following files are not malicious, but they are indicators because they were used during the attack.\r\n\r\nSHA256 hash:\r\n\r\n \t078163d5c16f64caa5a14784323fd51451b8c831c73396b967b4e35e6879937b\r\n \tFilename: psexec.exe\r\n \tFile description: Sysinternals PsExec \u2014 remote execution \/ lateral movement tool (observed downloaded from Sysinternals).\r\n\r\nSHA256 hash:\r\n\r\n \t996e68f2fe1c8bb091f34e9bf39fd34d95c3e21508def1f54098a1874bfb825e\r\n \tFilename: chisel.exe\r\n \tFile description: Chisel \u2014 SSH\/HTTPS tunneling tool (persistence via SSH tunnel observed).\r\n\r\nSHA256 hash:\r\n\r\n \t6784e652f304bf8e43b42c29ad8dd146dd384fa9536b9c6640dfbc370c3e78de\r\n \tFilename: s3browser-12-6-1.exe\r\n \tFile description: S3 Browser client \u2014 used to upload files to S3 (used for exfiltration attempts).\r\n\r\nSHA256 hash:\r\n\r\n \te451287843b3927c6046eaabd3e22b929bc1f445eec23a73b1398b115d02e4fb\r\n \tFilename: ADExplorer64.exe\r\n \tFile description: ADExplorer (Sysinternals) \u2014 Active Directory data browsing tool.\r\n\r\nSHA256 hash:\r\n\r\n \t088f2aced9ed60c2ce853b065f57691403459e1e0d167891d6849e1b58228173\r\n \tFilename: goon.zip\r\n \tFile description: Archive containing attacker tools (observed in S3 bucket).\r\n\r\nSHA256 hash:\r\n\r\n \t6e2c39d0c00a6a8eef33f9670f941a88c957d3c1e9496392beedc98af14269a2\r\n \tFilename: OfficeSetup.exe\r\n \tFile description: Microsoft Office installer\/setup executable (observed use by the attackers while attempting to access mailboxes\/PSTs).\r\n\r\nNetwork Based\r\n\r\n\r\n\r\nIP Address or Domain\r\nDescription\r\n\r\n\r\n162.125.3[.]18\r\nAssociated with Dropbox \u2014 destination IP address observed in firewall logs from the attackers' VM\r\n\r\n\r\n104.16.100[.]29\r\nAssociated with Dropbox \u2014 destination IP address observed in firewall logs from the attackers' VM\r\n\r\n\r\nupload[.]ee\r\nOnline cloud storage\r\n\r\n\r\nuploadnow[.]io\r\nOnline cloud storage\r\n\r\n\r\nlimewire[.]com\r\nOnline cloud storage\r\n\r\n\r\nwe[.]tl\r\nOnline cloud storage\r\n\r\n\r\ns3browser[.]com\r\nS3 browser, used for exfiltration\r\n\r\n\r\nsean-referrals-commissions-electricity.trycloudflare[.]com\r\nOnline cloud storage\r\n\r\n\r\nfast[.]com\r\nUsed by attackers to check the internet     <script type="text/javascript">
    ;(function(win, doc, style, timeout) {
    var STYLE_ID = 'at-body-style';
    function getParent() {
    return doc.getElementsByTagName('head')[0];
    }
    function addStyle(parent, id, def) {
        if (!parent) {
        return;
        }
        var style = doc.createElement('style');
        style.id = id;
        style.innerHTML = def;
        parent.appendChild(style);
    }
    function removeStyle(parent, id) {
        if (!parent) {
            return;
        }
        var style = doc.getElementById(id);
        if (!style) {
            return;
        }
        parent.removeChild(style);
    }
    addStyle(getParent(), STYLE_ID, style);
    setTimeout(function() {
    removeStyle(getParent(), STYLE_ID);
    }, timeout);
    }(window, document, "body {visibility:hidden !important}", 3000));
    </script>

<script type="text/javascript">
var isIE11 = !!navigator.userAgent.match(/Trident.*rv\:11\./);
if(isIE11){
    var polyfill = 'https://unit42.paloaltonetworks.com/wp-content/themes/unit42-v6/dist/scripts/polyfill.min.js';
    document.write('<script type="text/javascript" src="'+polyfill+'">\x3C/script>');
}
    /**
 * String.prototype.replaceAll() polyfill
 * https://gomakethings.com/how-to-replace-a-section-of-a-string-with-another-one-with-vanilla-js/
 * @author Chris Ferdinandi
 * @license MIT
 */
if (!String.prototype.replaceAll) {
	String.prototype.replaceAll = function(str, newStr){
		// If a regex pattern
		if (Object.prototype.toString.call(str).toLowerCase() === '[object regexp]') {
			return this.replace(str, newStr);
		}
		// If a string
		return this.replace(new RegExp(str, 'g'), newStr);
	};
}

    /*! lozad.js - v1.16.0 - 2020-09-06 */
!function(t,e){"object"==typeof exports&&"undefined"!=typeof module?module.exports=e():"function"==typeof define&&define.amd?define(e):t.lozad=e()}(this,function(){"use strict";
/**
   * Detect IE browser
   * @const {boolean}
   * @private
   */var g="undefined"!=typeof document&&document.documentMode,f={rootMargin:"0px",threshold:0,load:function(t){if("picture"===t.nodeName.toLowerCase()){var e=t.querySelector("img"),r=!1;null===e&&(e=document.createElement("img"),r=!0),g&&t.getAttribute("data-iesrc")&&(e.src=t.getAttribute("data-iesrc")),t.getAttribute("data-alt")&&(e.alt=t.getAttribute("data-alt")),r&&t.append(e)}if("video"===t.nodeName.toLowerCase()&&!t.getAttribute("data-src")&&t.children){for(var a=t.children,o=void 0,i=0;i<=a.length-1;i++)(o=a[i].getAttribute("data-src"))&&(a[i].src=o);t.load()}t.getAttribute("data-poster")&&(t.poster=t.getAttribute("data-poster")),t.getAttribute("data-src")&&(t.src=t.getAttribute("data-src")),t.getAttribute("data-srcset")&&t.setAttribute("srcset",t.getAttribute("data-srcset"));var n=",";if(t.getAttribute("data-background-delimiter")&&(n=t.getAttribute("data-background-delimiter")),t.getAttribute("data-background-image"))t.style.backgroundImage="url('"+t.getAttribute("data-background-image").split(n).join("'),url('")+"')";else if(t.getAttribute("data-background-image-set")){var d=t.getAttribute("data-background-image-set").split(n),u=d[0].substr(0,d[0].indexOf(" "))||d[0];// Substring before ... 1x

</script>

var(--wp--preset--gradient--blush-light-purple) !important;}.has-blush-bordeaux-gradient-background{background: var(--wp--preset--gradient--blush-bordeaux) !important;}.has-luminous-dusk-gradient-background{background: var(--wp--preset--gradient--luminous-dusk) !important;}.has-pale-ocean-gradient-background{background: var(--wp--preset--gradient--pale-ocean) !important;}.has-electric-grass-gradient-background{background: var(--wp--preset--gradient--electric-grass) !important;}.has-midnight-gradient-background{background: var(--wp--preset--gradient--midnight) !important;}.has-small-font-size{font-size: var(--wp--preset--font-size--small) !important;}.has-medium-font-size{font-size: var(--wp--preset--font-size--medium) !important;}.has-large-font-size{font-size: var(--wp--preset--font-size--large) !important;}.has-x-large-font-size{font-size: var(--wp--preset--font-size--x-large) !important;}
/*# sourceURL=global-styles-inline-css */
</style>
<link rel='stylesheet' id='wpdevart_lightbox_front_end_css-css' href='https://unit42.paloaltonetworks.com/wp-content/plugins/lightbox-popup/includes/style/wpdevart_lightbox_front.css?ver=6.9' media='all' />

<body class="wp-singular post-template-default single single-post postid-172362 single-format-standard wp-theme-unit42-v6 no-sidebar">

<nav id="navigation" class="site-nav collapse d-sm-block pb-20 mt-sm-10"  style="display: none!important;">
  <div class="container px-sm-30">
    <ul id="menu-primary-navigation" class="main-menu d-sm-flex font-weight-medium"><li id="menu-item-97290" class="menu-item menu-item-type-post_type menu-item-object-page menu-item-97290"><a href="https://unit42.paloaltonetworks.com/tools/">Tools</a></li>

<div class="panClean pan-template-home" id="main-nav-menu-cont" style="display:none;">
    <div class="cleanHeader mainNavigationComp baseComponent parbase">
        <div class="productNav2021Component dark default" id="PAN_2021_NAV_ASYNC">
        </div>

<!--  Start: Scripts Migrated From Unit42-v5 -->
<script type="text/javascript">
	function getCookie(cname) {
	 	var name = cname + "=";
  		var decodedCookie = decodeURIComponent(document.cookie);
		var ca = decodedCookie.split(';');
  		for(var i = 0; i <ca.length; i++) {
    			var c = ca[i];
    			while (c.charAt(0) == ' ') {
     				 c = c.substring(1);
    			}
    			if (c.indexOf(name) == 0) {
    				 return c.substring(name.length, c.length);
    			}
  		}
  		return "";
	}

	var referer = "";//sessionStorage.container;
	var pcontainer = sessionStorage.getItem("container");
	var searchResultsPagePath = "";
	if(((pcontainer) && pcontainer.indexOf('Prisma')!=-1)){
	    referer = 'Prisma' ;
	}        
        else if(((pcontainer) && pcontainer.indexOf('Cortex')!=-1)){
            if( pcontainer.indexOf('CloudCortex') != -1){
                referer = 'CloudCortex' ;
            }
            else{
                referer = 'Cortex' ;
            }	    
	}
        else if(((pcontainer) && pcontainer.indexOf('Sase')!=-1)){
	    referer = 'Sase' ;
	}
	else if(((pcontainer) && pcontainer.indexOf('Unit')!=-1)){
	    referer = 'Unit' ;
	}
	else if(((pcontainer) && pcontainer.indexOf('Ngfw')!=-1)){
	    referer = 'Ngfw' ;
	}
        var fromRef = document.referrer;
        var nContainer = getCookie("navContainer");
        if(nContainer){//If user is coming from main site, we need to reset the container		
		if(fromRef  && fromRef.indexOf("prismacloud.io")!=-1){
                        referer = 'Prisma' ;
                        sessionStorage.setItem("container","Prisma");
                } else if(fromRef.indexOf("paloaltonetworks.com")!=-1 || fromRef.indexOf("paloaltonetworks.jp")!=-1 ){
                        if(nContainer.indexOf('Prisma') != -1){
                            referer = 'Prisma' ;
                            sessionStorage.setItem("container","Prisma");
                        }
                        if(nContainer.indexOf('Cortex') != -1){
                            if( nContainer.indexOf('CloudCortex') != -1){
                                referer = 'CloudCortex';
                                sessionStorage.setItem("container","CloudCortex");
                            }
                            else{
                                referer = 'Cortex';
                                sessionStorage.setItem("container","Cortex");
                            }                            
                        }
			if(nContainer.indexOf('Sase') != -1){
                            referer = 'Sase' ;
                            sessionStorage.setItem("container","Sase");
                        }
			if(nContainer.indexOf('Unit') != -1){
                            referer = 'Unit' ;
                            sessionStorage.setItem("container","Unit");
                        }
			if(nContainer.indexOf('Ngfw') != -1){
                            referer = 'Ngfw' ;
                            sessionStorage.setItem("container","Ngfw");
                        }
			document.cookie = 'navContainer=; path=/; domain=.paloaltonetworks.com; expires=' + new Date(0).toUTCString();
		}
	}
if(referer != "Prisma" && referer != "CloudCortex" && referer != "Cortex" && referer != "Sase" && referer != "Unit" && referer != "Ngfw") {
    referer = 'Unit' ;
    sessionStorage.setItem("container","Unit");
}
function callMainSitePrismaNavHTML(){
    
    var referrer_domain = 'https://www.paloaltonetworks.com';
    sessionStorage.setItem("domain",referrer_domain);
    if(referer == 'Prisma'){
        //var menu_url = referrer_domain+'/_jcr_content/globals/cleanHeaderPrisma.prismaRenderer.html';
        var menu_url = 'https://unit42.paloaltonetworks.com/wp-content/themes/unit42-v6/unit-nav-renderer.php?type=prisma&domain=en';
        searchResultsPagePath = referrer_domain+"/search/prismasearch";
        }
    if(referer == 'CloudCortex'){
        var menu_url = 'https://unit42.paloaltonetworks.com/wp-content/themes/unit42-v6/unit-nav-renderer.php?type=cortexcloud&domain=en';
        searchResultsPagePath = referrer_domain+"/search/cortexcloudsearch";	
    }
    if(referer == 'Cortex'){
        //var menu_url = referrer_domain+'/_jcr_content/globals/cleanHeaderCortex.cortexRenderer.html';	
        var menu_url = 'https://unit42.paloaltonetworks.com/wp-content/themes/unit42-v6/unit-nav-renderer.php?type=cortex&domain=en';	
        searchResultsPagePath = referrer_domain+"/search/cortexsearch";	
    }
    if(referer == 'Sase'){
        //var menu_url = referrer_domain+'/_jcr_content/globals/cleanHeaderSase.saseRenderer.html';
        var menu_url = 'https://unit42.paloaltonetworks.com/wp-content/themes/unit42-v6/unit-nav-renderer.php?type=sase&domain=en';
        searchResultsPagePath = referrer_domain+"/search/sasesearch";
    }
    if(referer == 'Unit'){
        var menu_url = 'https://unit42.paloaltonetworks.com/wp-content/themes/unit42-v6/unit-nav-renderer.php?type=unit42&domain=en';
        searchResultsPagePath = referrer_domain+"/content/pan/en_US/search/unit42search";
		    }
    if(referer == 'Ngfw'){
        //var menu_url = 'https://unit42.paloaltonetworks.com/wp-content/themes/unit42-v6/ngfw-cdss-nav-renderer.php';
        var menu_url = 'https://unit42.paloaltonetworks.com/wp-content/themes/unit42-v6/unit-nav-renderer.php?type=ngfw&domain=en'; 
        searchResultsPagePath = referrer_domain+"/search/ngfwcdsssearch";
    }
    httpGet(menu_url,'menu_html');
    document.getElementById('main-nav-menu-cont').removeAttribute("style");
}
function addStyle(styles) {            
    /* Create style document */
    var css = document.createElement('style');
    css.type = 'text/css';

    if (css.styleSheet) 
        css.styleSheet.cssText = styles;
    else 
        css.appendChild(document.createTextNode(styles));

    /* Append style to the tag name */
    document.getElementsByTagName("head")[0].appendChild(css);
}
function httpGet(theUrl,req_type) {
    if (window.XMLHttpRequest) { // code for IE7+, Firefox, Chrome, Opera, Safari
        xmlhttp=new XMLHttpRequest();
    } else {// code for IE6, IE5
        xmlhttp=new ActiveXObject("Microsoft.XMLHTTP");
    }
    xmlhttp.onreadystatechange=function()
    {
        if (xmlhttp.readyState==4 && xmlhttp.status==200)
        {
            if(req_type == 'menu_html'){
            var nav_text = xmlhttp.responseText.replaceAll('https://static.cloud.coveo.com/searchui/v2.9159/js/CoveoJsSearch.Lazy.min.js', '');
            nav_text = nav_text.replaceAll('src="/', 'src="'+maindomain_lang+'/');
            nav_text = nav_text.replaceAll("'/content", "'"+maindomain_lang+"/content");
                        
            document.getElementById("PAN_2021_NAV_ASYNC").innerHTML = nav_text.replaceAll('href="/', 'href="'+maindomain_lang+'/');

            var lozad_back = document.getElementsByClassName('lozad-background');
            Array.prototype.forEach.call(lozad_back, function(el) {
            // Do stuff here
            var el_back_img_path = el.getAttribute('data-background-image');
            var first_pos = el_back_img_path.indexOf("'");
            var last_pos = el_back_img_path.indexOf("'",first_pos+1);
            el_back_img_path = el_back_img_path.substring(first_pos+1,last_pos);
            el.setAttribute("data-background-image",main_site_url+el_back_img_path);
            });
            const observer_lozad = lozad('.lozad, .lozad-background'); // lazy loads elements with default selector as '.lozad'
            observer_lozad.observe();
            }
            if(req_type == 'head_inline_css'){
            addStyle(xmlhttp.responseText);
            }
        }
    }
    xmlhttp.open("GET", theUrl, true );
    xmlhttp.send();    
}    
    
if(referer == 'Prisma' || referer == 'CloudCortex' || referer == 'Cortex' || referer == 'Sase' || referer == 'Unit' || referer == 'Ngfw'){
    const article = document.querySelector('#PAN_2021_NAV_ASYNC');
    if(referer == 'Prisma'){
        article.dataset.type = 'prisma';
        $('#PAN_2021_NAV_ASYNC').removeClass('default').addClass('defaultRedesigned');
    }
    else if(referer == 'CloudCortex'){
        article.dataset.type = 'cloudcortex';
    }
    else if(referer == 'Cortex'){
        article.dataset.type = 'cortex';
    }
    else if(referer == 'Sase'){
        article.dataset.type = 'sase';
    }
    else if(referer == 'Unit'){
        article.dataset.type = 'unit';
    }
    else if(referer == 'Ngfw'){
        article.dataset.type = 'ngfw';
    }
    //set class to default
    if(referer == 'Unit' || referer == 'Ngfw'){
        $('#PAN_2021_NAV_ASYNC').removeClass('default').addClass('defaultRedesigned');
    }
    callMainSitePrismaNavHTML();
}
</script>
<!--  End: Scripts Migrated From Unit42-v5 -->

	<div class="ab__share" id="shareDropdown" role="button" aria-expanded="false">
	<a href="#" role="link" title="Click here to share" data-page-track="true" data-page-track-value="muddled-libra-ops-playbook:share" class="">Share<img src="https://unit42.paloaltonetworks.com/wp-content/themes/unit42-v6/dist/images/icons/down-arrow.svg" alt="Down arrow"></a><ul class="share-dropdown" role="menu">

              </div>
            </div>
          </div>
        </div>
      </section> 
    <section class="section blog-contents"> 
      <div class="pa blog-editor">
        <div class="l-container">
          <div class="be__wrapper">
            <div class="be__contents">
              <div class="be__contents-wrapper">
                <h2><a id="post-172362-_4lt92rr5muov"></a>Executive Summary</h2>
<p>During a September 2025 incident response investigation, Unit 42 discovered a rogue virtual machine (VM) which we believe with high confidence to be used by the cybercrime group Muddled Libra (aka Scattered Spider, UNC3944). The contents of this rogue VM and activity from the attack provide valuable insight into the operational playbook of this threat actor.</p>
<p>Muddled Libra created the VM after the group successfully gained unauthorized access to the target's VMware vSphere environment. Activities during the attack include:</p>
<ul>
<li>Performing reconnaissance</li>
<li>Downloading tools</li>
<li>Establishing persistence via a command and control (C2) channel</li>
<li>Using stolen certificates</li>
<li>Copying files from the rogue VM to the target's domain controller (DC)</li>
<li>Interacting with the target’s Snowflake infrastructure</li>
</ul>
<p>Based on the characteristics of the attack, we assess with high confidence that Muddled Libra conducted it. This article provides a detailed analysis of our observations to shed further light on the threat actor’s tactics, techniques and procedures (TTPs).</p>
<p>Palo Alto Networks are better protected from the threats discussed in this article through the following products and services:</p>
<ul>
<li><a href="https://docs.paloaltonetworks.com/wildfire" target="_blank" rel="noopener">Advanced WildFire</a></li>
<li><a href="https://www.paloaltonetworks.com/cortex/cloud" target="_blank" rel="noopener">Cortex Cloud</a></li>
<li><a href="https://www.paloaltonetworks.com/cortex/cortex-xdr?_gl=1*13pmp8e*_ga*NzQyNjM2NzkuMTY2NjY3OTczNw..*_ga_KS2MELEEFC*MTY2OTczNjA2MS4zMS4wLjE2Njk3MzYwNjEuNjAuMC4w" target="_blank" rel="noopener">Cortex XDR</a> and <a href="https://www.paloaltonetworks.com/resources/datasheets/cortex-xsiam-aag" target="_blank" rel="noopener">XSIAM</a></li>
</ul>
<p>If you think you might have been compromised or have an urgent matter, contact the <a href="https://start.paloaltonetworks.com/contact-unit42.html" target="_blank" rel="noopener">Unit 42 Incident Response team</a>.</p>
<table style="width: 100%;">
<thead>
<tr>
<td style="width: 35%;"><b>Related Unit 42 Topics</b></td>
<td style="width: 100%;"><a href="https://unit42.paloaltonetworks.com/tag/muddled-libra/" target="_blank" rel="noopener"><b>Muddled Libra</b></a>, <strong><a href="https://unit42.paloaltonetworks.com/category/cybercrime/" target="_blank" rel="noopener">Cybercrime</a></strong></td>
</tr>
</thead>
</table>
<h2><a id="post-172362-_8ggiaq5gurhq"></a>Who Is Muddled Libra?</h2>
<p>As previously documented, threat actors affiliated with Muddled Libra use various <a href="https://unit42.paloaltonetworks.com/threat-group-assessment-muddled-libra-2024/" target="_blank" rel="noopener">social engineering tactics</a> (e.g., smishing, vishing) to gain initial access to targeted organizations. Activities can include targeting call centers operated by potential victims, as well as those outsourced to third-party firms. These third-party firms include business process outsourcing (BPOs) and managed service providers (MSPs). This expands the group’s range of potential targets.</p>
<p>Threat actors affiliated with Muddled Libra are highly proficient at exploiting human psychology by impersonating employees to attempt password and multi-factor authentication (MFA) resets. Figure 1 illustrates the composition of Muddled Libra in terms of their demographics, tradecraft, victim targeting and actions on objectives.</p>
<figure id="attachment_172387" aria-describedby="caption-attachment-172387" style="width: 1000px" class="wp-caption alignnone"><img  class="wp-image-172387 lozad"  data-src="https://unit42.paloaltonetworks.com/wp-content/uploads/2026/02/four-infographic-panels-illustrating-cyber-attack.png" alt="Four infographic panels illustrating cyber attack stages by Muddled Libra/Scattered Spider: 1. Demographics - focusing on Western-based, largely English fluent young entities, characterized as brash and destructive. 2. Targeting - involving business process outsourcing, telecommunications, financial services, and retail and hospitality. 3. Tradecraft - includes SIM swapping, social engineering, remote management, and ransomware. 4. Objectives - features external pivoting, intellectual property theft, cryptocurrency theft, and extortion via encryption. Logo of Palo Alto Networks and Unit 42 at the bottom right." width="1000" height="444" data-srcset="https://unit42.paloaltonetworks.com/wp-content/uploads/2026/02/four-infographic-panels-illustrating-cyber-attack.png 2048w, https://unit42.paloaltonetworks.com/wp-content/uploads/2026/02/four-infographic-panels-illustrating-cyber-attack-786x349.png 786w, https://unit42.paloaltonetworks.com/wp-content/uploads/2026/02/four-infographic-panels-illustrating-cyber-attack-1575x700.png 1575w, https://unit42.paloaltonetworks.com/wp-content/uploads/2026/02/four-infographic-panels-illustrating-cyber-attack-768x341.png 768w, https://unit42.paloaltonetworks.com/wp-content/uploads/2026/02/four-infographic-panels-illustrating-cyber-attack-1536x683.png 1536w" sizes="(max-width: 1000px) 100vw, 1000px" /><figcaption id="caption-attachment-172387" class="wp-caption-text">Figure 1. Muddled Libra threat profile.</figcaption></figure>
<p>While their tradecraft has evolved, threat actors affiliated with Muddled Libra continue to minimize their use of malware throughout the attack chain. Whenever possible, they prefer to use their targets' own assets against them.</p>
<p>Threat actors often abuse, take advantage of or subvert legitimate products for malicious purposes. This does not imply that the legitimate product is flawed or malicious.</p>
<h2><a id="post-172362-_wven14kmgum2"></a>Background on the Attack Chain</h2>
<p>We assisted a client with an incident response engagement in September 2025. Throughout our investigation, we identified and recovered a VM created and leveraged by the threat actor to conduct the early stages of its attack. Attackers were unable to delete this VM before their access was cut off. We often observe threat actors creating VMs within targeted environments to avoid detection from endpoint tools like endpoint detection and response (EDR) or extended detection and response (XDR).</p>
<p>By examining this VM, we discovered the tools the attackers leveraged and the basic troubleshooting they conducted during their unauthorized access. This provided insights into their operational methods.</p>
<p>Using forensic artifacts and logs, we uncovered a large amount of activity conducted from this VM, including lateral movement and tools used. Figure 2 further illustrates our observations during the investigation. Note that the analysis below is of a single system and does not cover the entire incident observed by Unit 42.</p>
<figure id="attachment_172398" aria-describedby="caption-attachment-172398" style="width: 1000px" class="wp-caption alignnone"><img  class="wp-image-172398 lozad"  data-src="https://unit42.paloaltonetworks.com/wp-content/uploads/2026/02/word-image-173361-172362-2.png" alt="Diagram outlining a cyber-attack process. It includes six steps: initial access through a Help Desk breach, obtaining Azure and Citrix credentials, establishing a remote connection, performing Active Directory reconnaissance, using Snowflake queries for data exfiltration, and uploading a PST file from a rogue virtual machine. Annotations highlight each phase, with an &quot;Incident Response Actions Initiated&quot; banner at the top right." width="1000" height="444" data-srcset="https://unit42.paloaltonetworks.com/wp-content/uploads/2026/02/word-image-173361-172362-2.png 1421w, https://unit42.paloaltonetworks.com/wp-content/uploads/2026/02/word-image-173361-172362-2-786x349.png 786w, https://unit42.paloaltonetworks.com/wp-content/uploads/2026/02/word-image-173361-172362-2-768x341.png 768w" sizes="(max-width: 1000px) 100vw, 1000px" /><figcaption id="caption-attachment-172398" class="wp-caption-text">Figure 2. High-level chain of events in the attack investigated by Unit 42.</figcaption></figure>
<h2><a id="post-172362-_50343o6a6han"></a>A Peek Into Muddled Libra Tactics</h2>
<p>Approximately two hours after gaining initial access to the target’s environment, we observed the attackers accessing the target’s vSphere portal and creating a new VM named “New Virtual Machine.” The attackers then leveraged this VM for the early stages of the incident as a beachhead host using the local Administrator account.</p>
<p>Shortly after logging into the newly created VM, attackers downloaded stolen certificates from the targeted environment. They leveraged these certificates to forge tickets throughout their attack chain.</p>
<p>Within three minutes, attackers established additional persistence in the target’s environment using an SSH tunnel through the <a href="https://github.com/jpillora/chisel" target="_blank" rel="noopener">Chisel</a> tool. This tool was contained in a ZIP archive named <span style="font-family: 'courier new', courier, monospace;">goon.zip</span> that was hosted on an AWS S3 bucket under the attackers' control.</p>
<p>Nearly one minute after they downloaded the ZIP archive containing Chisel, we observed malicious network connections to an attacker-controlled IP address over TCP port 443 (HTTPS). We observed this connection for a total of 15 hours. Figures 3-5 illustrate these observations.</p>
<figure id="attachment_172409" aria-describedby="caption-attachment-172409" style="width: 1000px" class="wp-caption alignnone"><img  class="wp-image-172409 lozad"  data-src="https://unit42.paloaltonetworks.com/wp-content/uploads/2026/02/word-image-175657-172362-3.png" alt="Screenshot of code related to ZoneTransfer, showing ZoneId equals 3 and a partially redacted HostUrl with an AWS link." width="1000" height="138" data-srcset="https://unit42.paloaltonetworks.com/wp-content/uploads/2026/02/word-image-175657-172362-3.png 1265w, https://unit42.paloaltonetworks.com/wp-content/uploads/2026/02/word-image-175657-172362-3-786x108.png 786w, https://unit42.paloaltonetworks.com/wp-content/uploads/2026/02/word-image-175657-172362-3-768x106.png 768w" sizes="(max-width: 1000px) 100vw, 1000px" /><figcaption id="caption-attachment-172409" class="wp-caption-text">Figure 3. URL hosting archive containing the Chisel tool.</figcaption></figure>
<figure id="attachment_172420" aria-describedby="caption-attachment-172420" style="width: 1000px" class="wp-caption alignnone"><img  class="wp-image-172420 lozad"  data-src="https://unit42.paloaltonetworks.com/wp-content/uploads/2026/02/word-image-177580-172362-4.png" alt="A computer interface displaying two file paths with some information redacted. " width="1000" height="102" data-srcset="https://unit42.paloaltonetworks.com/wp-content/uploads/2026/02/word-image-177580-172362-4.png 2048w, https://unit42.paloaltonetworks.com/wp-content/uploads/2026/02/word-image-177580-172362-4-786x80.png 786w, https://unit42.paloaltonetworks.com/wp-content/uploads/2026/02/word-image-177580-172362-4-1920x196.png 1920w, https://unit42.paloaltonetworks.com/wp-content/uploads/2026/02/word-image-177580-172362-4-768x78.png 768w, https://unit42.paloaltonetworks.com/wp-content/uploads/2026/02/word-image-177580-172362-4-1536x157.png 1536w" sizes="(max-width: 1000px) 100vw, 1000px" /><figcaption id="caption-attachment-172420" class="wp-caption-text">Figure 4. The downloaded tool, <span style="font-family: 'courier new', courier, monospace;">goon.zip</span>.</figcaption></figure>
<figure id="attachment_172431" aria-describedby="caption-attachment-172431" style="width: 1000px" class="wp-caption alignnone"><img  class="wp-image-172431 lozad"  data-src="https://unit42.paloaltonetworks.com/wp-content/uploads/2026/02/word-image-179593-172362-5.png" alt="A screenshot showing two file paths in a computer terminal." width="1000" height="113" data-srcset="https://unit42.paloaltonetworks.com/wp-content/uploads/2026/02/word-image-179593-172362-5.png 2048w, https://unit42.paloaltonetworks.com/wp-content/uploads/2026/02/word-image-179593-172362-5-786x89.png 786w, https://unit42.paloaltonetworks.com/wp-content/uploads/2026/02/word-image-179593-172362-5-1920x217.png 1920w, https://unit42.paloaltonetworks.com/wp-content/uploads/2026/02/word-image-179593-172362-5-768x87.png 768w, https://unit42.paloaltonetworks.com/wp-content/uploads/2026/02/word-image-179593-172362-5-1536x173.png 1536w" sizes="(max-width: 1000px) 100vw, 1000px" /><figcaption id="caption-attachment-172431" class="wp-caption-text">Figure 5. The SSH tunneling tool, <span style="font-family: 'courier new', courier, monospace;">chisel.exe</span>, extracted from <span style="font-family: 'courier new', courier, monospace;">goon.zip</span>.</figcaption></figure>
<p>A minute later, we observed them logging in interactively with a new local user account named <span style="font-family: 'courier new', courier, monospace;">gooner</span>.</p>
<p>Approximately 15 minutes after creating the VM, the attackers began using vSphere to power down two of the target's virtualized DCs. They then mounted the virtual machine disks (VMDKs) of the powered-down DCs. This allowed them to copy the <span style="font-family: 'courier new', courier, monospace;">NTDS.dit</span> and SYSTEM registry hive files from these two DCs and place them on the desktop of the Administrator account on their newly created VM.</p>
<p>Approximately two minutes later, they wrote two files, <span style="font-family: 'courier new', courier, monospace;">result</span> and <span style="font-family: 'courier new', courier, monospace;">result.kerb</span>, to the local Administrator account’s desktop. We retrieved these files and determined that these were decrypted versions of the target’s <span style="font-family: 'courier new', courier, monospace;">NTDS.dit</span> Active Directory database, which contained hashes of all users. Figures 6 and 7 illustrate these observations.</p>
<figure id="attachment_172442" aria-describedby="caption-attachment-172442" style="width: 1000px" class="wp-caption alignnone"><img  class="wp-image-172442 lozad"  data-src="https://unit42.paloaltonetworks.com/wp-content/uploads/2026/02/word-image-181686-172362-6.png" alt="A screenshot of a virtual machine log file showing a sequence of events related to power off and disk operations. Some sensitive information is redacted." width="1000" height="391" data-srcset="https://unit42.paloaltonetworks.com/wp-content/uploads/2026/02/word-image-181686-172362-6.png 2048w, https://unit42.paloaltonetworks.com/wp-content/uploads/2026/02/word-image-181686-172362-6-786x307.png 786w, https://unit42.paloaltonetworks.com/wp-content/uploads/2026/02/word-image-181686-172362-6-1792x700.png 1792w, https://unit42.paloaltonetworks.com/wp-content/uploads/2026/02/word-image-181686-172362-6-768x300.png 768w, https://unit42.paloaltonetworks.com/wp-content/uploads/2026/02/word-image-181686-172362-6-1536x600.png 1536w" sizes="(max-width: 1000px) 100vw, 1000px" /><figcaption id="caption-attachment-172442" class="wp-caption-text">Figure 6. VMware logs of the shutdown activities of the DC.</figcaption></figure>
<figure id="attachment_172453" aria-describedby="caption-attachment-172453" style="width: 1000px" class="wp-caption alignnone"><img  class="wp-image-172453 lozad"  data-src="https://unit42.paloaltonetworks.com/wp-content/uploads/2026/02/word-image-184975-172362-7.png" alt="Screenshot of a file directory showing paths under &quot;Users\Administrator\Desktop.&quot; The files listed are &quot;ntds.dit,&quot; &quot;SYSTEM,&quot; &quot;result,&quot; and &quot;result.kerb.&quot;" width="1000" height="147" data-srcset="https://unit42.paloaltonetworks.com/wp-content/uploads/2026/02/word-image-184975-172362-7.png 1377w, https://unit42.paloaltonetworks.com/wp-content/uploads/2026/02/word-image-184975-172362-7-786x116.png 786w, https://unit42.paloaltonetworks.com/wp-content/uploads/2026/02/word-image-184975-172362-7-768x113.png 768w" sizes="(max-width: 1000px) 100vw, 1000px" /><figcaption id="caption-attachment-172453" class="wp-caption-text">Figure 7. List of files discovered for credential dump, NTML hash and Kerberos hash.</figcaption></figure>
<p>At nearly 30 minutes of access to this newly created VM, the attackers began executing the Active Directory enumeration tool <a href="https://github.com/adrecon/ADRecon" target="_blank" rel="noopener">ADRecon</a>. We observed and retrieved dozens of files associated with ADRecon, including a PowerShell script and output files.</p>
<p>These files contained information such as:</p>
<ul>
<li>Domain details</li>
<li>Forest</li>
<li>Trusts</li>
<li>Sites</li>
<li>Subnets</li>
<li>Schema</li>
<li>Password policy</li>
<li>DCs</li>
<li>Service Principal Names (SPNs)</li>
<li>Users</li>
<li>Group Policy Objects (GPOs)</li>
</ul>
<p>The output of the ADRecon tool would then be placed in a ZIP archive named <span style="font-family: 'courier new', courier, monospace;">&lt;VICTIM ORGANIZATION&gt;.zip</span> (where <span style="font-family: 'courier new', courier, monospace;">&lt;victim organization&gt;</span> represents the name of the victim, redacted for this report). We also observed the attackers downloading the tool <span style="font-family: 'courier new', courier, monospace;">ADExplorer64.exe</span> directly from the Microsoft SysInternals domain. Figure 8 illustrates these observations.</p>
<figure id="attachment_172464" aria-describedby="caption-attachment-172464" style="width: 1000px" class="wp-caption alignnone"><img  class="wp-image-172464 lozad"  data-src="https://unit42.paloaltonetworks.com/wp-content/uploads/2026/02/word-image-186983-172362-8.png" alt="Screenshot of a file directory showing numerous CSV files. A highlighted ZIP archive at the bottom labeled &quot;&lt;Redacted&gt;.zip&quot; is pointed out with text noting it's named after a victim organization." width="1000" height="592" data-srcset="https://unit42.paloaltonetworks.com/wp-content/uploads/2026/02/word-image-186983-172362-8.png 2048w, https://unit42.paloaltonetworks.com/wp-content/uploads/2026/02/word-image-186983-172362-8-743x440.png 743w, https://unit42.paloaltonetworks.com/wp-content/uploads/2026/02/word-image-186983-172362-8-1182x700.png 1182w, https://unit42.paloaltonetworks.com/wp-content/uploads/2026/02/word-image-186983-172362-8-768x455.png 768w, https://unit42.paloaltonetworks.com/wp-content/uploads/2026/02/word-image-186983-172362-8-1536x910.png 1536w" sizes="(max-width: 1000px) 100vw, 1000px" /><figcaption id="caption-attachment-172464" class="wp-caption-text">Figure 8. List of ADRecon output files discovered during our investigation.</figcaption></figure>
<p>Within the ADRecon output, the threat actors only opened the CSV file <span style="font-family: 'courier new', courier, monospace;">ComputerSPNs.csv</span>. This file contained all available service principal names (SPNs) associated with hosts in the environment. Attackers gather this information to help identify critical services running that they are interested in targeting. These critical services include:</p>
<ul>
<li>Veeam</li>
<li>Terminal services</li>
<li>Hyper-V</li>
<li>MSSQL</li>
<li>Exchange</li>
<li>Other similar systems as shown in Figure 9</li>
</ul>
<figure id="attachment_172475" aria-describedby="caption-attachment-172475" style="width: 1000px" class="wp-caption alignnone"><img  class="wp-image-172475 lozad"  data-src="https://unit42.paloaltonetworks.com/wp-content/uploads/2026/02/word-image-191642-172362-9.png" alt="Table displaying columns labeled &quot;User Name,&quot; &quot;Name,&quot; &quot;Service,&quot; and &quot;Host.&quot; Multiple rows mention services like &quot;MSSQLSvc,&quot; &quot;Microsoft Virtual Console Service.&quot; " width="1000" height="500" data-srcset="https://unit42.paloaltonetworks.com/wp-content/uploads/2026/02/word-image-191642-172362-9.png 2048w, https://unit42.paloaltonetworks.com/wp-content/uploads/2026/02/word-image-191642-172362-9-786x393.png 786w, https://unit42.paloaltonetworks.com/wp-content/uploads/2026/02/word-image-191642-172362-9-1401x700.png 1401w, https://unit42.paloaltonetworks.com/wp-content/uploads/2026/02/word-image-191642-172362-9-768x384.png 768w, https://unit42.paloaltonetworks.com/wp-content/uploads/2026/02/word-image-191642-172362-9-1536x767.png 1536w" sizes="(max-width: 1000px) 100vw, 1000px" /><figcaption id="caption-attachment-172475" class="wp-caption-text">Figure 9. List of targeted services discovered during our investigation.</figcaption></figure>
<p>One hour later, attackers began searching the web for various acronyms associated with the targeted organization, likely to determine what data could be sensitive and interesting for exfiltration. This included searches such as “what is NAIC code” and “NAICS code lookup,” as shown in Figure 10.</p>
<p>A North American Industry Classification System (NAICS) code is a six-digit number that classifies businesses by their primary economic activity. By looking up this code, attackers might have been trying to understand the business category of the target organization.</p>
<figure id="attachment_172486" aria-describedby="caption-attachment-172486" style="width: 451px" class="wp-caption alignnone"><img  class="wp-image-172486 lozad"  data-src="https://unit42.paloaltonetworks.com/wp-content/uploads/2026/02/word-image-194282-172362-10.png" alt="Search queries displayed: &quot;what is naic code - Search&quot; and &quot;naics code lookup - Search.&quot;" width="451" height="89" /><figcaption id="caption-attachment-172486" class="wp-caption-text">Figure 10. Example of web searches.</figcaption></figure>
<p>Thirty minutes after their web searches, attackers began interacting with significant data from the target’s Snowflake database, which they also downloaded to their VM. For the next few hours, attackers began interacting with the data and attempting to identify ways to send the data from their VM to a file-sharing site. However, we observed them having difficulties finding a file-sharing site that the targeted organization had not already blocked.</p>
<p>After trying several common file-sharing sites, they began using Bing to search on the phrases “upload files” and “upload files no registration” to identify a file-sharing site that was not blocked. We observed attempts at accessing sites such as:</p>
<ul>
<li>LimeWire</li>
<li><span style="font-family: 'courier new', courier, monospace;">upload[.]ee</span></li>
<li><span style="font-family: 'courier new', courier, monospace;">uploadnow[.]io</span></li>
<li><span style="font-family: 'courier new', courier, monospace;">filetransfer[.]io</span></li>
<li><span style="font-family: 'courier new', courier, monospace;">filebin[.]io</span></li>
<li>Dropbox</li>
</ul>
<p>Figures 11-13 illustrate these observations.</p>
<figure id="attachment_172497" aria-describedby="caption-attachment-172497" style="width: 1000px" class="wp-caption alignnone"><img  class="wp-image-172497 lozad"  data-src="https://unit42.paloaltonetworks.com/wp-content/uploads/2026/02/word-image-196234-172362-11.png" alt="A screenshot of a webpage displaying a list of URLs. Many entries are related to &quot;Snowflake&quot; with paths including &quot;/Marketing/Data/Database&quot; and &quot;/Research/hquery/&quot;. Sections are redacted with black boxes. Multiple entries include options labeled &quot;Search&quot; or &quot;Table&quot;." width="1000" height="462" data-srcset="https://unit42.paloaltonetworks.com/wp-content/uploads/2026/02/word-image-196234-172362-11.png 1559w, https://unit42.paloaltonetworks.com/wp-content/uploads/2026/02/word-image-196234-172362-11-786x364.png 786w, https://unit42.paloaltonetworks.com/wp-content/uploads/2026/02/word-image-196234-172362-11-1514x700.png 1514w, https://unit42.paloaltonetworks.com/wp-content/uploads/2026/02/word-image-196234-172362-11-768x355.png 768w, https://unit42.paloaltonetworks.com/wp-content/uploads/2026/02/word-image-196234-172362-11-1536x710.png 1536w" sizes="(max-width: 1000px) 100vw, 1000px" /><figcaption id="caption-attachment-172497" class="wp-caption-text">Figure 11. List of Snowflake web browsing activities documented from our investigation.</figcaption></figure>
<figure id="attachment_172508" aria-describedby="caption-attachment-172508" style="width: 549px" class="wp-caption alignnone"><img  class="wp-image-172508 lozad"  data-src="https://unit42.paloaltonetworks.com/wp-content/uploads/2026/02/word-image-199210-172362-12.png" alt="Screenshot of search results on how to upload files without registering with a service. " width="549" height="282" /><figcaption id="caption-attachment-172508" class="wp-caption-text">Figure 12. Web searches for cloud storage services discovered during our investigation.</figcaption></figure>
<figure id="attachment_172519" aria-describedby="caption-attachment-172519" style="width: 550px" class="wp-caption alignnone"><img  class="wp-image-172519 lozad"  data-src="https://unit42.paloaltonetworks.com/wp-content/uploads/2026/02/word-image-201081-172362-13.png" alt="Screenshot of search results on file uploading and storage services where access is blocked. " width="550" height="186" data-srcset="https://unit42.paloaltonetworks.com/wp-content/uploads/2026/02/word-image-201081-172362-13.png 1046w, https://unit42.paloaltonetworks.com/wp-content/uploads/2026/02/word-image-201081-172362-13-786x266.png 786w, https://unit42.paloaltonetworks.com/wp-content/uploads/2026/02/word-image-201081-172362-13-768x260.png 768w" sizes="(max-width: 550px) 100vw, 550px" /><figcaption id="caption-attachment-172519" class="wp-caption-text">Figure 13. Web browsing activities to cloud storage services discovered during our investigation.</figcaption></figure>
<p>Shortly after interacting with the data, the attackers began lateral movement using multiple then-compromised accounts with their SSH tunnel, RDP and PsExec. They downloaded the PsExec tool directly from the Microsoft SysInternals domain.</p>
<p>Approximately four hours after the creation of their VM, the attackers began looking for additional sensitive data. At that time, we observed them having compromised a handful of accounts, one of which they used to access the mailboxes of other accounts to download a Personal Storage Table (PST) file. However, based on Bing search history, they had difficulty accessing the mailbox via Office.</p>
<p>The attackers then searched the web for “<span style="font-family: 'courier new', courier, monospace;">office[.]com</span> old setup download” and “is there a place to download an older version of Outlook?” Attackers also reviewed various Reddit posts related to this query. Shortly after, they downloaded and ran <span style="font-family: 'courier new', courier, monospace;">OfficeSetup.exe</span> from Microsoft.</p>
<p>They then began troubleshooting items such as “Outlook slow downloading emails” and performed several internet speed checks using the site <span style="font-family: 'courier new', courier, monospace;">fast[.]com</span>. Figures 14-17 illustrate these observations.</p>
<figure id="attachment_172530" aria-describedby="caption-attachment-172530" style="width: 1000px" class="wp-caption alignnone"><img  class="wp-image-172530 lozad"  data-src="https://unit42.paloaltonetworks.com/wp-content/uploads/2026/02/word-image-203315-172362-14.png" alt="Screenshot of Putlook login attempts. " width="1000" height="431" data-srcset="https://unit42.paloaltonetworks.com/wp-content/uploads/2026/02/word-image-203315-172362-14.png 1172w, https://unit42.paloaltonetworks.com/wp-content/uploads/2026/02/word-image-203315-172362-14-786x339.png 786w, https://unit42.paloaltonetworks.com/wp-content/uploads/2026/02/word-image-203315-172362-14-768x331.png 768w" sizes="(max-width: 1000px) 100vw, 1000px" /><figcaption id="caption-attachment-172530" class="wp-caption-text">Figure 14. Online Outlook login activities discovered during our investigation.</figcaption></figure>
<figure id="attachment_172541" aria-describedby="caption-attachment-172541" style="width: 1000px" class="wp-caption alignnone"><img  class="wp-image-172541 lozad"  data-src="https://unit42.paloaltonetworks.com/wp-content/uploads/2026/02/word-image-205556-172362-15.png" alt="Screenshot of search results for installing or reinstalling Outlook, looking for &quot;classic&quot; versions or the 2016 version. " width="1000" height="60" data-srcset="https://unit42.paloaltonetworks.com/wp-content/uploads/2026/02/word-image-205556-172362-15.png 2048w, https://unit42.paloaltonetworks.com/wp-content/uploads/2026/02/word-image-205556-172362-15-786x47.png 786w, https://unit42.paloaltonetworks.com/wp-content/uploads/2026/02/word-image-205556-172362-15-1920x114.png 1920w, https://unit42.paloaltonetworks.com/wp-content/uploads/2026/02/word-image-205556-172362-15-768x46.png 768w, https://unit42.paloaltonetworks.com/wp-content/uploads/2026/02/word-image-205556-172362-15-1536x92.png 1536w" sizes="(max-width: 1000px) 100vw, 1000px" /><figcaption id="caption-attachment-172541" class="wp-caption-text">Figure 15. Web searches for Outlook agents discovered during our investigation.</figcaption></figure>
<figure id="attachment_172552" aria-describedby="caption-attachment-172552" style="width: 1000px" class="wp-caption alignnone"><img  class="wp-image-172552 lozad"  data-src="https://unit42.paloaltonetworks.com/wp-content/uploads/2026/02/word-image-207688-172362-16.png" alt="A computer screen displaying a list of search queries related to downloading older versions of Outlook and Office.com setup files. The queries mention search terms like &quot;reddit&quot; and &quot;microsoft&quot; and include parts of URLs with strings of letters and numbers." width="1000" height="275" data-srcset="https://unit42.paloaltonetworks.com/wp-content/uploads/2026/02/word-image-207688-172362-16.png 1518w, https://unit42.paloaltonetworks.com/wp-content/uploads/2026/02/word-image-207688-172362-16-786x216.png 786w, https://unit42.paloaltonetworks.com/wp-content/uploads/2026/02/word-image-207688-172362-16-768x211.png 768w" sizes="(max-width: 1000px) 100vw, 1000px" /><figcaption id="caption-attachment-172552" class="wp-caption-text">Figure 16. Web searches for older Outlook versions discovered during our investigation.</figcaption></figure>
<figure id="attachment_172563" aria-describedby="caption-attachment-172563" style="width: 626px" class="wp-caption alignnone"><img  class="wp-image-172563 lozad"  data-src="https://unit42.paloaltonetworks.com/wp-content/uploads/2026/02/word-image-210011-172362-17.png" alt="Search history dropdown showing three searches: &quot;speedtest,&quot; and two identical entries for &quot;outlook slow downloading emails.&quot;" width="626" height="128" /><figcaption id="caption-attachment-172563" class="wp-caption-text">Figure 17. Web searches to troubleshoot slow email download issues and perform a speed test.</figcaption></figure>
<p>In addition to troubleshooting their download speeds, attackers used Bing to identify the location of the Outlook ODT file. Figure 18 illustrates this observation.</p>
<figure id="attachment_172574" aria-describedby="caption-attachment-172574" style="width: 457px" class="wp-caption alignnone"><img  class="wp-image-172574 lozad"  data-src="https://unit42.paloaltonetworks.com/wp-content/uploads/2026/02/word-image-211846-172362-18.png" alt="The image shows a search browser tab with the query &quot;outlook odt file location.&quot;" width="457" height="42" /><figcaption id="caption-attachment-172574" class="wp-caption-text">Figure 18. Web search for the Outlook ODT file location discovered during our investigation.</figcaption></figure>
<p>Additionally, we observed Microsoft Defender, presumably installed on the VM by default, taking action on multiple malicious files on this VM. This included ADRecon, Chisel and GoSecretsDump. Figure 19 lists these detections.</p>
<figure id="attachment_172585" aria-describedby="caption-attachment-172585" style="width: 1000px" class="wp-caption alignnone"><img  class="wp-image-172585 lozad"  data-src="https://unit42.paloaltonetworks.com/wp-content/uploads/2026/02/word-image-213654-172362-19.png" alt="A screenshot displaying a file directory the describes malware names and their description. " width="1000" height="60" data-srcset="https://unit42.paloaltonetworks.com/wp-content/uploads/2026/02/word-image-213654-172362-19.png 2048w, https://unit42.paloaltonetworks.com/wp-content/uploads/2026/02/word-image-213654-172362-19-786x47.png 786w, https://unit42.paloaltonetworks.com/wp-content/uploads/2026/02/word-image-213654-172362-19-1920x115.png 1920w, https://unit42.paloaltonetworks.com/wp-content/uploads/2026/02/word-image-213654-172362-19-768x46.png 768w, https://unit42.paloaltonetworks.com/wp-content/uploads/2026/02/word-image-213654-172362-19-1536x92.png 1536w" sizes="(max-width: 1000px) 100vw, 1000px" /><figcaption id="caption-attachment-172585" class="wp-caption-text">Figure 19. Microsoft Defender detections on tools discovered during our investigation.</figcaption></figure>
<p>After some time, the attackers then began attempting to exfiltrate the Outlook PST file. They first searched for the <a href="https://s3browser.com/" target="_blank" rel="noopener">S3 Browser</a> tool. Once they downloaded the tool from the S3 Browser website, attackers then attempted to exfiltrate the PST file by uploading it to their S3 bucket. Figures 20 and 21 illustrate these observations.</p>
<figure id="attachment_172596" aria-describedby="caption-attachment-172596" style="width: 1000px" class="wp-caption alignnone"><img  class="wp-image-172596 lozad"  data-src="https://unit42.paloaltonetworks.com/wp-content/uploads/2026/02/word-image-216006-172362-20.png" alt="Screenshot of file paths for two EXE files. " width="1000" height="66" data-srcset="https://unit42.paloaltonetworks.com/wp-content/uploads/2026/02/word-image-216006-172362-20.png 2048w, https://unit42.paloaltonetworks.com/wp-content/uploads/2026/02/word-image-216006-172362-20-786x52.png 786w, https://unit42.paloaltonetworks.com/wp-content/uploads/2026/02/word-image-216006-172362-20-1920x128.png 1920w, https://unit42.paloaltonetworks.com/wp-content/uploads/2026/02/word-image-216006-172362-20-768x51.png 768w, https://unit42.paloaltonetworks.com/wp-content/uploads/2026/02/word-image-216006-172362-20-1536x102.png 1536w" sizes="(max-width: 1000px) 100vw, 1000px" /><figcaption id="caption-attachment-172596" class="wp-caption-text">Figure 20. Downloaded S3 browser executable discovered during our investigation.</figcaption></figure>
<figure id="attachment_172607" aria-describedby="caption-attachment-172607" style="width: 1000px" class="wp-caption alignnone"><img  class="wp-image-172607 lozad"  data-src="https://unit42.paloaltonetworks.com/wp-content/uploads/2026/02/word-image-218189-172362-21.png" alt="Screenshot of brwoser logs with multiple portions redacted. " width="1000" height="352" data-srcset="https://unit42.paloaltonetworks.com/wp-content/uploads/2026/02/word-image-218189-172362-21.png 1914w, https://unit42.paloaltonetworks.com/wp-content/uploads/2026/02/word-image-218189-172362-21-786x277.png 786w, https://unit42.paloaltonetworks.com/wp-content/uploads/2026/02/word-image-218189-172362-21-768x270.png 768w, https://unit42.paloaltonetworks.com/wp-content/uploads/2026/02/word-image-218189-172362-21-1536x541.png 1536w" sizes="(max-width: 1000px) 100vw, 1000px" /><figcaption id="caption-attachment-172607" class="wp-caption-text">Figure 21. S3 Browser logs of PST file upload.</figcaption></figure>
<p>After approximately 15 hours of access to their VM, attackers began browsing various VMware ESXi hosts. They continued to pivot through the network, leveraging access to multiple compromised accounts before their access was terminated by the targeted organization’s security team. Figure 22 illustrates this observation.</p>
<figure id="attachment_172618" aria-describedby="caption-attachment-172618" style="width: 1000px" class="wp-caption alignnone"><img  class="wp-image-172618 lozad"  data-src="https://unit42.paloaltonetworks.com/wp-content/uploads/2026/02/word-image-221180-172362-22.png" alt="Screenshot of browser logs with multiple portions redacted. " width="1000" height="576" data-srcset="https://unit42.paloaltonetworks.com/wp-content/uploads/2026/02/word-image-221180-172362-22.png 1170w, https://unit42.paloaltonetworks.com/wp-content/uploads/2026/02/word-image-221180-172362-22-764x440.png 764w, https://unit42.paloaltonetworks.com/wp-content/uploads/2026/02/word-image-221180-172362-22-768x442.png 768w" sizes="(max-width: 1000px) 100vw, 1000px" /><figcaption id="caption-attachment-172618" class="wp-caption-text">Figure 22. Browsing activities to the target’s VMware ESXi hosts discovered during our investigation.</figcaption></figure>
<h2><a id="post-172362-_yi0b1v4vud22"></a>Conclusion</h2>
<p>Intrusion operations Muddled Libra conducts have affected the business operations of many organizations across the globe. This is not because they use advanced malware or novel exploits, but because they exploit the weakest link: humans.</p>
<p>While focusing on identity compromise and social engineering, this threat actor leverages legitimate tools and existing infrastructure to blend in. They operate quietly and maintain persistence.</p>
<p>This incident offers a rare window into an operational playbook used by Muddled Libra, revealing how a single rogue VM can serve as a powerful foothold for lateral movement and data theft. The threat actor's methods underscore the importance of:</p>
<ul>
<li>Strengthening identity security</li>
<li>Enforcing strict access controls</li>
<li>Continuously monitoring for anomalous use of administrative tools and cloud environments</li>
</ul>
<p>Organizations should adopt a defense-in-depth strategy centered on:</p>
<ul>
<li>Protecting identity</li>
<li>Maintaining least-privileged access</li>
<li>Detecting living-off-the-land behaviors</li>
</ul>
<p>While Muddled Libra’s tactics may appear simple, their effectiveness reminds us that cybersecurity resilience begins not with complexity, but with vigilance, visibility and disciplined access management.</p>
<p>Palo Alto Networks customers are better protected from the threats discussed above through the following products:</p>
<p>The <a href="https://docs.paloaltonetworks.com/wildfire" target="_blank" rel="noopener">Advanced WildFire</a> machine-learning models and analysis techniques have been reviewed and updated in light of the indicators shared in this research.</p>
<p><a href="https://www.paloaltonetworks.com/cortex/cloud" target="_blank" rel="noopener">Cortex Cloud</a> customers can better protect their cloud infrastructure from the topics discussed within this article through the proper placement of Cortex Cloud <a href="https://docs-cortex.paloaltonetworks.com/r/Cortex-CLOUD/Cortex-Cloud-Runtime-Security-Documentation/Endpoint-protection" target="_blank" rel="noopener">XDR endpoint agent</a> and <a href="https://docs-cortex.paloaltonetworks.com/r/Cortex-XSIAM/Cortex-XSIAM-Premium-Documentation/Use-cases" target="_blank" rel="noopener">serverless agents</a> within their cloud environment. Designed to protect a cloud’s posture and runtime operations against these threats, Cortex Cloud helps detect and prevent the malicious operations or configuration alterations or exploitations discussed within this article.</p>
<p><a href="https://www.paloaltonetworks.com/cortex/cortex-xdr" target="_blank" rel="noopener">Cortex XDR</a> and <a href="https://www.paloaltonetworks.com/resources/datasheets/cortex-xsiam-aag" target="_blank" rel="noopener">XSIAM</a> help to prevent the threats described in this blog, by employing the <a href="https://docs-cortex.paloaltonetworks.com/r/Cortex-XDR/Cortex-XDR-4.x-Documentation/Malware-protection" target="_blank" rel="noopener">Malware Prevention Engine</a>. This approach combines several layers of protection, including <a href="https://docs.paloaltonetworks.com/wildfire" target="_blank" rel="noopener">WildFire</a>, Behavioral Threat Protection and the Local Analysis module, designed to prevent both known and unknown malware from causing harm to endpoints.</p>
<p>If you think you may have been compromised or have an urgent matter, get in touch with the <a href="https://start.paloaltonetworks.com/contact-unit42.html" target="_blank" rel="noopener">Unit 42 Incident Response team</a> or call:</p>
<ul>
<li>North America: Toll Free: +1 (866) 486-4842 (866.4.UNIT42)</li>
<li>UK: +44.20.3743.3660</li>
<li>Europe and Middle East: +31.20.299.3130</li>
<li>Asia: +65.6983.8730</li>
<li>Japan: +81.50.1790.0200</li>
<li>Australia: +61.2.4062.7950</li>
<li>India: 000 800 050 45107</li>
<li>South Korea: +82.080.467.8774</li>
</ul>
